methodsAttempted.notes: Official university pages were fetched from Academic Integrity, the Generative AI work group site, DoIT, and CELT.
Departmental and catalog paths were checked but not used for broad institutional claims.

artifacts.summary: Official Stony Brook sources show no central university AI policy yet, but provide AI-specific academic integrity, privacy, tool, and teaching guidance. Departmental and poorly extracted catalog sources were not used for university-wide claims.

stopConditions: Stop after official Stony Brook central, Academic Integrity, DoIT, and CELT sources have enough evidence for narrow claims.
Do not use departmental or external sources for university-wide binding claims.
Do not bypass login walls, CAPTCHAs, or robots restrictions.

artifacts.sourceTitle: Resources | Generative AI at Stony Brook
AI Tools | Division of Information Technology
AI (Artificial Intelligence) | Center for Excellence in Learning and Teaching

artifacts.claimText: Stony Brook's central generative AI FAQ states that the University does not currently have an AI policy and is reviewing existing policies for generative AI implications.

citation.citationTitle: Resources | Generative AI at Stony Brook

artifacts.claimText: Stony Brook's Academic Integrity Policy lists representing work generated by artificial intelligence as one's own work as an example of academic dishonesty.

Stony Brook guidance says generative AI use in coursework can be prohibited, allowed, or required depending on the course or assignment, and students should consult course policies or instructors when unsure.

citation.citationTitle: Resources | Generative AI at Stony Brook

artifacts.claimText: Stony Brook guidance warns users not to enter sensitive, personal, or proprietary information into generative AI tools without understanding the protections provided by the tool.

citation.citationTitle: Resources | Generative AI at Stony Brook

artifacts.claimText: Stony Brook DoIT maintains an AI Tools directory that identifies available tools such as Copilot, Gemini, NotebookLM, Turnitin, and Zoom AI Companion, while noting that listed tools may not be used with HIPAA data.

citation.citationTitle: AI Tools | Division of Information Technology
citation.publisher: Stony Brook University Division of Information Technology

artifacts.claimText: Stony Brook CELT guidance advises instructors to discuss AI usage policies clearly, include AI statements in syllabi, and outline which assignments allow or do not allow AI tools.

citation.citationTitle: AI (Artificial Intelligence) | Center for Excellence in Learning and Teaching
citation.publisher: Stony Brook University Center for Excellence in Learning and Teaching

artifacts.claimText: Stony Brook CELT guidance says users should review AI-generated content for accuracy because AI tools can produce biased, illogical, false, or nonexistent-source outputs.

citation.citationTitle: AI (Artificial Intelligence) | Center for Excellence in Learning and Teaching
citation.publisher: Stony Brook University Center for Excellence in Learning and Teaching

artifacts.sourceTitle: Resources | Generative AI at Stony Brook
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: Does Stony Brook have an AI policy? No. The University is reviewing existing policies to ensure they appropriately address the power and implications of generative AI.
artifacts.evidenceSnippetDisplay: The central FAQ says there is no AI policy yet and that policy owners are reviewing existing policies for generative AI implications.
artifacts.evidenceLocator: FAQ item: Does Stony Brook have an AI policy?

citation.citationTitle: Resources | Generative AI at Stony Brook

artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: The following represents examples of academic dishonesty and does not constitute an exhaustive list: ... Representing work generated by artificial intelligence as one's own work.
artifacts.evidenceSnippetDisplay: The Academic Integrity Policy expressly lists representing AI-generated work as one's own as an example of academic dishonesty.

artifacts.sourceTitle: Resources | Generative AI at Stony Brook
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: Using generative AI in academic work is prohibited in some cases, allowable in others, and required in some instances. Read policies on academic integrity ... consult course policies, and when in doubt ask your instructor.
artifacts.evidenceSnippetDisplay: The FAQ frames coursework AI use as course- and assignment-dependent and advises students to consult policies or instructors.
artifacts.evidenceLocator: FAQ item: Can I use generative AI tools in my coursework?

citation.citationTitle: Resources | Generative AI at Stony Brook

artifacts.sourceTitle: Resources | Generative AI at Stony Brook
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: You should not enter sensitive, personal, or proprietary information into a generative AI tool without understanding the specific protections provided by that tool.
artifacts.evidenceSnippetDisplay: The FAQ warns against entering sensitive, personal, or proprietary information into AI tools without understanding the protections.
artifacts.evidenceLocator: FAQ item: Are there privacy concerns with generative AI?

citation.citationTitle: Resources | Generative AI at Stony Brook

artifacts.sourceTitle: AI Tools | Division of Information Technology
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: A current directory of AI tools offered by DoIT ... HIPPA Data Considerations. The following tools may not be used with HIPAA data. Copilot ... Gemini ... NotebookLM ... Turnitin ... Zoom.
artifacts.evidenceSnippetDisplay: DoIT's AI Tools directory lists several tools and flags that the listed tools may not be used with HIPAA data.

citation.citationTitle: AI Tools | Division of Information Technology
citation.publisher: Stony Brook University Division of Information Technology

artifacts.sourceTitle: AI (Artificial Intelligence) | Center for Excellence in Learning and Teaching
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: It is important to discuss your policies on AI usage in a clear and unambiguous manner. Including an AI statement in your course syllabus ... outline which assignments allow for the use of AI tools and which assignments do not permit the usage of AI tools.
artifacts.evidenceSnippetDisplay: CELT recommends clear syllabus and assignment-level AI expectations.

citation.citationTitle: AI (Artificial Intelligence) | Center for Excellence in Learning and Teaching
citation.publisher: Stony Brook University Center for Excellence in Learning and Teaching

artifacts.sourceTitle: AI (Artificial Intelligence) | Center for Excellence in Learning and Teaching
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: In many cases, AI tools can develop biased, illogical, or false information and even can generate sources that do not exist. It is important that anyone who makes use of AI tools to generate content reviews its output for accuracy.
artifacts.evidenceSnippetDisplay: CELT warns that AI can generate false or biased outputs and says users should review AI output for accuracy.

citation.citationTitle: AI (Artificial Intelligence) | Center for Excellence in Learning and Teaching
citation.publisher: Stony Brook University Center for Excellence in Learning and Teaching

artifacts.notes: Approved as source_status only; does not assert that no related policies exist.
Approved because the policy explicitly names work generated by artificial intelligence.
Approved with course-specific wording to avoid overclaiming a blanket rule.
Approved as privacy/data-handling guidance supported by central FAQ language.
Approved as a directory/tool availability claim, not procurement or universal approval.
Approved as CELT teaching guidance, not binding policy.
Approved as general teaching/usage guidance about AI output accuracy review.

artifacts.title: Stony Brook University generative AI policy source review
artifacts.summary: Stony Brook's official sources indicate no central AI policy yet, while Academic Integrity, the central Generative AI FAQ, DoIT, and CELT provide narrow guidance on AI-generated work, coursework permissions, sensitive data, available tools, and syllabus/accuracy practices.
limitations: This tracker is not legal advice, not academic integrity advice, and not an official university statement unless a linked source is the university's own official page.
Departmental Technology and Society material was rejected for university-wide claims because it appears department-scoped.
Graduate catalog generative AI path was not used because the fetched text did not expose substantive AI-specific content.
artifacts.sourceRightsPolicy: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.
